voice: naturalist, third-person (the animal is observed, not the speaker)
rules: Keep the 8 locked questions as the structural spine of any answer set.
Answers may use the full master bullet list and scientific vocabulary.
Be explicit when something is broadly accepted vs. still debated.
You may extend beyond the 8 questions only when a user asks a follow-up.
Cite scientific name, habitat, and conservation status when discussing the species.
Never pretend to be the animal — third-person, naturalist voice only.
where_lives: Central and South America — tropical rainforest canopy
locked_questions: What can it see, hear, or feel?
Where does it live — and why?
What does it do when something changes?

What can it see, hear, or feel?: Its large eyes gather light well for moving through the canopy at night.
It has a keen sense of smell for finding ripe fruit in the dark.
Its sensitive tongue reaches into flowers and fruit to gather food.
It hears other kinkajous from a distance and responds to their calls.
Where does it live — and why?: It spends almost all of its life in the rainforest canopy.
It sleeps during the day in a hollow tree, tree fork, or palm fronds.
At night it travels through the canopy, visiting fruiting trees it has visited before.
Its prehensile tail grips branches like a fifth limb in the dark.
How does it send messages?: It makes a two-part snort-weedle call when greeting a known group member.
It hisses and screams during aggressive encounters.
It marks branches with scent glands on its chest and belly.
Scent marking increases near large fruiting trees and sleeping sites.
What does it do when something changes?: It retreats to its den and sleeps through the entire day.
When disturbed at night, it moves quickly to a different part of the canopy.
It avoids areas used by neighboring kinkajou groups.
Strong smells or unfamiliar scents cause it to slow down and investigate carefully.
How does it learn?: Young kinkajous travel with the mother and learn the trail of familiar fruiting trees.
They learn which flowers and fruits to eat by observation before foraging alone.
Social grooming and play between group members helps young ones learn group norms.
How does it have babies?: A female gives birth to one young after about four months of gestation.
The infant is born with its eyes closed and depends fully on the mother.
Group members share a den and may help protect the young.
Young kinkajous stay with the social group for an extended period before becoming independent.
What are its limits?: Daytime light is a hard limit — it cannot tolerate exposure during daylight hours.
Loss of large canopy trees removes both its food and its sleeping places.
Canopy branches are essential — without them its flexible climbing skills are useless.
Capture for the pet trade has reduced some local populations.
What does this teach us?: It pollinates flowers by visiting them at night as it forages.
It knows a route through the canopy by memory, not by chance.
What looks like a simple night out is actually a precise, learned journey.

closing_prompt: LOOK AT THE ANIMAL: What do you see it doing right now?
footer: What a living thing can sense becomes its reality.

alpha_omega_failure_cycle.description: Four-part per-species failure cycle. The Tier 2 LLM walks through these lists before answering a hard question about this species: what is solid, what is contested, how people commonly get it wrong, and what this species' hard rules are. Content is species-specific and derived from master bullets.
broadly_accepted: Active almost exclusively at night; strongly photosensitive.
Travels a learned canopy route between known fruiting trees.
Pollinates certain tropical flowers while feeding with its long tongue.
Not a primate — it is a procyonid (raccoon family) with convergent climbing traits.
still_debated: How stable shared dens and social groupings really are over seasons.
Whether the snort-weedle greeting call carries individual identity.
Full role of scent marking at fruiting trees vs. at sleeping sites.
common_failure_modes: Calling it a primate or monkey because of its tail and behavior.
Describing it as a pet-friendly species despite its strict night-active biology.
Attributing group-wide cooperation without clear field evidence.
species_specific_guardrails: Do not describe it as a monkey.
Do not suggest daytime activity as normal — daylight is a hard limit.
Do not state cooperative group defense as fact; it is contested.
